agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 services aligned to bounded contexts: Orders, Courier Operations (assignment + location tracking), Payments, Notifications, and Customer Accounts. The split is driven by three forces: (1) deployment coupling — payments and location tracking have radically different change cadences and risk profiles, (2) scaling independence — location tracking at 5K writes/sec needs a fundamentally different data store and scaling strategy than payment processing, and (3) team cognitive load — 8 engineers across 5 services is tight but viable with 2-person ownership pairs and shared on-call. The key architectural bet is merging courier assignment and location tracking into one Courier Operations service rather than splitting them — they share the same aggregate (Courier) and splitting would create a distributed consistency problem for no deployment gain.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, status transitions, cancellation. Owns the Order aggregate including line items, delivery address, and order state machine. Emits domain events on state changes (OrderPlaced, OrderPickedUp, OrderDelivered).","boundaries":"Knows nothing about courier identity or location. Receives courier assignment events from Courier Operations. Does not call Payments directly — emits OrderPlaced and lets Payments react."},{"name":"Courier Operations Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment/dispatch, route optimization, and real-time location ingestion. Owns the Courier aggregate (availability, current location, active assignment). Ingests ~5K location writes/sec at peak. Runs assignment algorithm when OrderPlaced events arrive.","boundaries":"Owns all location data and courier state. Exposes a query API for current courier position (consumed by Orders for ETA and by Notifications for customer-facing tracking). Does not know about payments or customer accounts."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment authorization, capture, refunds, and ledger. Owns the Payment aggregate with strict transactional consistency. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe, Adyen, etc.).","boundaries":"Triggered by domain events (OrderPlaced → authorize, OrderDelivered → capture, RefundRequested → refund). Never called synchronously from the order placement hot path — uses a saga pattern for authorization. Owns its own database with ACID guarantees. No eventual consistency compromises on money."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Orchestrates customer-facing notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Subscribes to domain events from all other services and applies notification rules (e.g., OrderPickedUp → push notification to customer).","boundaries":"Stateless routing layer with a thin persistence layer for notification preferences and delivery status tracking. Has no business logic — purely reactive. If this service goes down, orders and payments continue unaffected."},{"name":"Customer Account Service","responsibility":"Customer profiles, authentication, addresses, preferences. Owns the Customer aggregate.","boundaries":"Other services reference customers by ID only. No service embeds customer details — they query this service or consume a cached projection. Low change frequency — likely the last service to need independent scaling."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Operations Service","contract":"Domain event: OrderPlaced { orderId, pickupAddress, deliveryAddress, requiredBy }. Courier Operations subscribes and initiates assignment. Courier Operations emits CourierAssigned { orderId, courierId, estimatedPickup } back.","coupling_type":"event (async)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Domain event: OrderPlaced { orderId, customerId, amount, paymentMethodToken }. Payment Service authorizes and emits PaymentAuthorized or PaymentFailed. Order Service listens and transitions order state accordingly.","coupling_type":"event (async, saga)"},{"from":"Courier Operations Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"Domain events: CourierAssigned, CourierArrivedAtPickup, OrderPickedUp, OrderDelivered. Order Service updates order status.","coupling_type":"event (async)"},{"from":"Notification Service","to":"All Services","contract":"Subscribes to: OrderPlaced, CourierAssigned, OrderPickedUp, OrderDelivered, PaymentFailed, RefundCompleted. Applies notification rules. No upstream service knows about Notification Service.","coupling_type":"event (async, downstream consumer)"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"All Services","contract":"REST/gRPC. Gateway routes by path prefix: /orders/*, /couriers/*, /payments/*, /accounts/*. Location tracking endpoint: /couriers/{id}/location uses WebSocket or SSE for real-time updates.","coupling_type":"synchronous (query path only)"},{"from":"Courier Operations Service","to":"Customer Account Service","contract":"Sync query: GET /accounts/{customerId}/address for delivery address verification. Cached aggressively. Fallback: use address embedded in the OrderPlaced event.","coupling_type":"synchronous (query, cached)"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → API Gateway → Order Service creates Order (PENDING). 2. Order Service emits OrderPlaced → consumed by Payment Service (authorize) and Courier Operations (assign). 3. Payment Service authorizes → emits PaymentAuthorized → Order Service transitions to CONFIRMED. If PaymentFailed, Order Service transitions to CANCELLED (saga compensation). 4. Courier Operations runs assignment algorithm → emits CourierAssigned → Order Service updates with courier info. 5. Courier app sends GPS updates every 10s → Courier Operations ingests via dedicated location endpoint (UDP or lightweight HTTP) → writes to time-series store. 6. Courier arrives, picks up, delivers → state events flow to Order Service and Notification Service. 7. OrderDelivered → Payment Service captures authorized payment. 8. Notification Service reacts to all major events, sends push/SMS/email per customer preferences.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture with Domain Events","rationale":"Primary integration pattern. Eliminates synchronous coupling between services. Order placement doesn't block on payment authorization or courier assignment. Each service reacts independently."},{"pattern":"Saga (Choreography-based)","rationale":"Order placement spans Order + Payment. Rather than a distributed transaction, Order emits OrderPlaced, Payment reacts with PaymentAuthorized/PaymentFailed, Order compensates on failure. Choreography over orchestration because we have only 2 participants — an orchestrator would be over-engineering."},{"pattern":"CQRS (limited, for location tracking)","rationale":"Location writes (5K/sec) go to an append-only time-series store optimized for writes. Location reads for customer tracking and ETA come from a materialized view optimized for point queries. Separate write and read models within Courier Operations only — not a system-wide CQRS."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (migration strategy)","rationale":"Don't rewrite everything at once. Extract Payment Service first (highest risk if coupled, strict consistency needs make it a clean seam). Then Courier Operations (highest scaling pressure). Then Notifications, Customer Accounts, and finally Orders. Each extraction wraps the monolith incrementally."},{"pattern":"Database per Service","rationale":"Each service owns its datastore. Courier Operations uses a time-series DB (TimescaleDB or InfluxDB) for location + Postgres for courier state. Payment uses Postgres with strict ACID. Others use Postgres. No shared databases."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for clients. Handles auth, rate limiting, and routing. Keeps clients decoupled from service topology."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED → ASSIGNED → PICKED_UP → DELIVERED → CANCELLED, contains line items, addresses, references courierId and paymentId by ID only)"]},{"name":"Courier Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier (root: courierId, contains: currentLocation, availability status, activeAssignmentId, vehicle type)","Assignment (root: assignmentId, contains: orderId, courierId, route, status, timestamps)","LocationPoint (value object: lat, lng, timestamp, courierId — stored in time-series, not a traditional aggregate)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, contains: orderId, amount, currency, status [AUTHORIZED, CAPTURED, REFUNDED, FAILED], paymentMethodToken, gatewayTransactionId)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationPreference (root: customerId, contains: channel preferences per event type)","NotificationLog (root: notificationId, contains: channel, status, attempts)"]},{"name":"Customer Accounts","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Customer (root: customerId, contains: profile, addresses, authentication credentials, payment methods)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Orders defines what needs to be delivered; Courier Operations conforms to the OrderPlaced event schema but owns all assignment logic."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Orders publishes OrderPlaced with payment intent; Payments conforms to the event and owns all payment processing logic."},{"upstream":"All Core Contexts","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist. Notifications consumes events from all upstream contexts and conforms to their schemas. No influence on upstream models."},{"upstream":"Customer Accounts","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Shared Kernel (customer ID and basic address schema). Minimal shared types — only customerId as a reference and a standardized Address value object."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Merge courier assignment and location tracking into one Courier Operations service rather than splitting them","gains":"Avoids distributed consistency problem — assignment algorithm needs real-time location to assign nearest courier. Single service can query location data locally with sub-millisecond latency. Simpler operationally for 8-person team.","sacrifices":"Larger service with two scaling concerns (write-heavy location ingestion + compute-heavy assignment algorithm). May eventually need to split if team grows.","alternatives_considered":["Separate Location Tracking Service — rejected because assignment would need synchronous calls to Location at high frequency, creating tight coupling and latency","Location as a shared data layer — rejected because shared databases violate service autonomy"]},{"decision":"Choreography-based saga over orchestration for order-payment flow","gains":"No central orchestrator to maintain. Two-party saga is simple enough for choreography. Each service owns its own reaction logic.","sacrifices":"Harder to visualize the overall flow. If we add more saga participants later (e.g., inventory, fraud checks), choreography becomes tangled.","alternatives_considered":["Orchestration saga with a dedicated saga coordinator — viable if we grow beyond 2-3 saga participants, but premature now"]},{"decision":"Event-driven async integration as the primary pattern (over synchronous REST calls between services)","gains":"Services are independently deployable and can fail independently. Notification Service downtime doesn't block orders. Natural buffering during traffic spikes.","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — there will be brief windows where Order shows PENDING while payment is already authorized. Harder to debug distributed flows without good observability tooling.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST between services — rejected because it re-creates monolithic coupling at the network level","GraphQL federation — interesting for the query side but doesn't solve the write-side coupling problem"]},{"decision":"Extract Payment Service first in the migration sequence","gains":"Highest-risk component (money). Clean seam — payment has clear inputs/outputs and minimal queries from other services. Validates the team's ability to operate a distributed system on a lower-traffic, well-understood domain.","sacrifices":"Doesn't address the immediate scaling pressure on location tracking. Team may feel urgency to extract Courier Operations first.","alternatives_considered":["Extract Courier Operations first — tempting due to scaling pressure, but riskier to learn microservices on the highest-throughput component. Better to build operational muscle on Payments first."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) requires only changes to the Notification Service. No upstream services need to change — they emit the same domain events."},{"area":"Assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"Algorithm lives entirely within Courier Operations. Can evolve from simple nearest-courier to ML-based optimization without touching any other service."},{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"Gateway integration is encapsulated in Payment Service. Can switch from Stripe to Adyen or add multi-gateway routing without any other service knowing."},{"area":"Location ingestion protocol","evolution_path":"Can start with HTTP POST, evolve to WebSocket or MQTT for lower overhead as courier fleet grows. Change is isolated to Courier Operations and the courier mobile app."},{"area":"Event broker","evolution_path":"Start with a managed broker (Amazon SNS/SQS, Google Pub/Sub). Can migrate to Kafka if event replay or stream processing becomes important. Services communicate via events — broker is an infrastructure detail."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Domain event schemas","reason":"Once published, event schemas become contracts. Breaking changes require versioning and consumer migration. Invest in schema design upfront — use a schema registry."},{"area":"Customer ID as the cross-cutting reference","reason":"Every service references customers by ID. Changing the ID format or introducing a different identity model would be a system-wide migration."},{"area":"Order state machine","reason":"Order states (PENDING → CONFIRMED → ASSIGNED → PICKED_UP → DELIVERED) are referenced by multiple services for their event reactions. Adding or renaming states requires coordinated updates."},{"area":"Payment consistency model","reason":"Strict ACID on payment transactions is non-negotiable. Cannot relax this for performance. If payment volumes grow, must scale vertically or shard — cannot go eventually consistent."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Operational maturity gap: team has run a monolith for 3 years and is now operating 5 services, a message broker, and an API gateway. Distributed systems debugging, deployment pipelines per service, and distributed tracing are new skills.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Extract incrementally via Strangler Fig. Start with Payment Service only. Run it alongside the monolith for 2-3 months before extracting the next service. Invest in observability from day 1: distributed tracing (Jaeger/Datadog), centralized logging, and a service mesh or at minimum health check dashboards."},{"risk":"Saga failure handling: if PaymentAuthorized is emitted but the Order Service misses the event (broker issue, service down), the order stays in PENDING while money is authorized.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement idempotent event handlers with at-least-once delivery. Use an outbox pattern for reliable event publishing. Add a reconciliation job that detects orphaned authorizations and either completes or voids them."},{"risk":"Location data volume: 5K writes/sec is manageable today but grows linearly with courier fleet. 5,000 couriers would mean 50K writes/sec.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a time-series database from the start (TimescaleDB, InfluxDB). Batch location updates on the courier app (send 3-5 points per request instead of one). Implement TTL-based data expiration — no need to keep raw GPS points beyond 30 days."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution: as the system matures, event schemas will need to change. Without a versioning strategy, consumers break.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Adopt schema versioning from the start. Use a schema registry (e.g., AWS Glue Schema Registry, Confluent Schema Registry). Design events with optional fields for forward compatibility. Never remove or rename fields — add new ones."},{"risk":"Team spread too thin: 8 engineers across 5 services means some services have single-owner risk.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Pair ownership: 2 engineers per service with overlap. Notifications and Customer Accounts are low-change — one pair can own both. Core services (Orders, Courier Ops, Payments) each get a dedicated pair. That's 8 engineers across 4 ownership pairs."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (months 1-3): Extract Payment Service. Build the event infrastructure (message broker, outbox pattern, schema registry). Establish CI/CD per service, distributed tracing, and centralized logging. Monolith still handles everything else. Phase 2 (months 3-6): Extract Courier Operations Service. Migrate location tracking to a time-series DB. This relieves the primary scaling bottleneck. Phase 3 (months 6-9): Extract Notification Service (low risk, purely reactive) and Customer Account Service. Phase 4 (months 9-12): Extract Order Service last — it's the most connected component and benefits from all the integration infrastructure built in earlier phases. The monolith becomes a thin shell and is eventually retired.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-service-decomposition.md — Architecture Decision Record for the decomposition strategy","docs/adr/002-event-driven-integration.md — ADR for choosing events over sync REST","docs/adr/003-saga-pattern-order-payment.md — ADR for choreography saga","docs/domain-events/event-catalog.md — schema definitions for all domain events","infra/docker-compose.yml — local development environment with all services + broker","services/payment-service/ — first extracted service (scaffold)","services/courier-operations-service/ — second extraction","services/notification-service/ — third extraction","services/customer-account-service/ — fourth extraction","services/order-service/ — final extraction from monolith","shared/event-schemas/ — schema definitions shared via registry, not code dependency"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service follows hexagonal architecture internally: domain core with no framework dependencies, application layer for use cases, infrastructure adapters for DB/messaging/HTTP. Events are published via the transactional outbox pattern — write to an outbox table in the same transaction as the domain change, then a relay process publishes to the broker. All event handlers are idempotent. Each service has its own CI/CD pipeline, database migration tooling, and health check endpoint.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Distributed monolith: if services call each other synchronously for every operation, you've just added network latency to your monolith. Default to async events.","Shared database: under no circumstances should two services read from or write to the same database. This is the #1 way teams fail at microservices.","Big bang migration: do not try to extract all services at once. The Strangler Fig approach is essential for a team learning distributed systems.","Premature event sourcing: CQRS for location tracking is warranted; full event sourcing across all services is not. Start with simple state + events.","Ignoring observability: the first service extraction must include distributed tracing, centralized logging, and alerting. Without it, debugging distributed failures is impossible."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition aligns cleanly with bounded contexts","Payment Service extraction as the first step is well-supported by risk analysis","Event-driven integration is the right default for this domain","Merging assignment and location tracking avoids a distributed consistency trap"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact event broker choice depends on the team's cloud provider and existing infrastructure — not specified","Whether 8 engineers is truly sufficient depends on their skill distribution and DevOps maturity","The choreography saga is right for 2 participants but may need to become orchestrated if fraud/inventory checks are added"],"assumptions":["The team has or can quickly adopt CI/CD per service","A managed message broker is available (cloud-hosted SNS/SQS, Pub/Sub, or managed Kafka)","The courier mobile app can be updated to point to new location tracking endpoints","The monolith uses a single relational database that can be logically partitioned before physical extraction"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 services aligned to bounded contexts: Orders, Courier Operations (assignment + location tracking), Payments, Notifications, and Customer Accounts. The split is driven by three forces: (1) deployment coupling — payments and location tracking have radically different change cadences and risk profiles, (2) scaling independence — location tracking at 5K writes/sec needs a fundamentally different data store and scaling strategy than
